authoritative_sources.title: Clothes Captioning: Complying with the Care Labeling Rule
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC is the primary U.S. authority for care-label requirements. The guidance defines covered products, permanent-label expectations, and the requirement for reliable evidence supporting care instructions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: label-pack review, care-instruction evidence validation, and exception refusal
used_for: refusal when care instructions lack a reasonable basis

authoritative_sources.title: Apparel and Labeling
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC's official topic page is authoritative for textile, wool, care-label, and leather-guide boundaries, including which fields belong on labels and how footwear sits within FTC guidance.

authoritative_sources.title: Clothing Business Guidance
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CPSC is the primary U.S. product-safety regulator for clothing flammability, children's apparel lead and phthalate rules, and tracking-label requirements. This guidance ties those obligations to artifact and testing expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: children's-product safety screening, test-evidence review, and escalation for missing safety records
used_for: refusal when safety evidence is missing

authoritative_sources.title: CPC Example – Clothing
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CPSC's CPC example is authoritative for the structure and minimum fields of children's clothing certification records and clarifies the finished-product versus component-evidence boundary.

authoritative_sources.title: ISO 8559-2:2025 - Designation of clothes sizes
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO is the primary international standards body, and ISO 8559-2 is the recognized public reference for apparel size designation using primary and secondary body-measurement indicators.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: size-spec review, POM alignment, and terminology normalization

authoritative_sources.title: ISO 9407:2019 - Footwear sizing — Mondopoint system of sizing and marking
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO 9407 is the authoritative public reference for footwear size designation and marking using Mondopoint, which materially changes footwear workflows relative to apparel sizing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: footwear-size mapping and label or carton-data review

authoritative_sources.title: Supporting Documents
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: H&M's public supplier portal is official named-industry operating guidance showing the real apparel artifact set used in technical support workflows, including care keys, artwork guidelines, technical documents, variable-data instructions, and size charts.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: label-pack, artwork, size-chart, and variable-data artifact handling
used_for: systems-of-record expectations for label and artwork support

authoritative_sources.title: Product Safety and Quality Assurance Tools and Processes
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Target's official product-safety and quality page is named-industry retailer guidance covering approval samples, production-readiness meetings, third-party testing, label validation, staff training, record keeping, and recall handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: sample approval, production-readiness screening, and testing-evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: What kind of information is linked to the GS1 Identification Key?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1 is the recognized standards body for trade-item identification. This article is authoritative for variant-aware master data such as size and colour, which directly affects apparel and footwear SKU, label, and carton workflows.
used_for: traceability across label and carton records

authoritative_sources.title: MRSL
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ZDHC is the widely recognized apparel and footwear chemical-management framework. Its MRSL is authoritative for upstream chemical restrictions, supplier communication, and pre-production material approval expectations.

authoritative_sources.title: OEKO-TEX STANDARD 100
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OEKO-TEX STANDARD 100 is a globally recognized textile and leather evidence-control benchmark for harmful-substance testing from yarn to finished product, including component-level coverage and annual review of substance limits.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: component and finished-product harmful-substance evidence review
used_for: annual refresh posture for chemical thresholds

workflow_stages.name: request intake and technical-design prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize style, product category, sample stage, market or jurisdiction, age grade, buyer or brand requirements, and system-of-record context before any support output is produced.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: tech-pack, measurement, and variant evidence review
workflow_stages.description: Compare the PLM tech pack, BOM, colorway matrix, POM table, grade rules, sample-measurement records, and GTIN or size master data to determine which artifact is authoritative.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: sample, fit, and construction issue handling
workflow_stages.description: Assess fit-session comments, recurring sample failures, construction clarifications, and sample-calendar impacts while preserving the boundary against final fit or pattern approval.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: label, safety, and chemical-evidence control
workflow_stages.description: Check care labels, fiber-content and origin fields, children's-product safety evidence, tracking-label data, and chemical-control records before a recommendation is allowed to advance.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: approval, closeout, and adjacent-lane escalation
workflow_stages.description: Close issues only when the artifact set, approvals, and evidence chain are complete, and escalate whenever technical-design, compliance, sourcing, costing, or release authority changes the safe answer.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: PLM tech packs, sketches, revision logs, bills of materials, trim lists, colorway matrices, and construction callouts
point-of-measure tables, size specs, grade rules, sample-measurement sheets, fit comments, and wear-test notes
sample requests, proto or fit or pre-production sample trackers, approval chains, factory clarification logs, and pre-production meeting notes
label artwork files, care instructions, fiber-content and origin fields, translation requests, hangtag data, and carton or packaging copy
CPSC flammability, lead, paint, phthalate, and tracking-label records; CPC or GCC artifacts; third-party lab reports
chemical-compliance evidence such as ZDHC or OEKO-TEX references, trim or material declarations, and supplier test summaries
development calendars, launch dates, cost-impact notes, MOQ flags, and owner-routing maps
systems_of_record: PLM or product data management system
laboratory information or compliance documentation repository

allowed: Classify technical-design support requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, system-of-record links, and authoritative records required to continue safely.
Produce evidence-backed resolution notes, stakeholder communications, sample-readiness summaries, and escalation packets while authority remains in lane.
Normalize apparel, textile, and footwear terminology into the controlled artifact set and preserve uncertainty when the record chain conflicts.
escalate: The request requires final technical-design fit approval, pattern or grading ownership, sourcing approval, compliance signoff, or release authority.
Care-label, product-safety, chemical, or buyer-manual requirements are missing or materially conflict across sources.
Recurring sample failures or repeated label or compliance blockers lack a named corrective owner.
Tenant policy, retailer guidance, and public baseline disagree in a way that changes whether the issue can be advanced.
refuse: Approve or imply approval for fit, pattern, grading, compliance, labeling, costing, factory booking, or shipment release without the required evidence and owners.
Bypass care-label, children's-product safety, tracking-label, chemical, or approval controls to protect schedule or customer launch dates.
Treat the industry name as cosmetic and answer with generic support language that ignores tech packs, size systems, samples, labels, or compliance records.

domain_failure_modes: a stale tech pack or sample-measurement sheet is treated as current and the factory updates the wrong pattern or sample
size-designation logic for apparel is incorrectly applied to footwear, causing label, carton, or GTIN variant errors
care-label or origin content is changed without the reliable evidence or buyer approval needed for the actual market
children's-product or tracking-label evidence is missing, but the lane still implies the style is ready to advance
repeat fit or construction failures are treated as routine clarifications instead of being escalated to formal technical ownership
retailer or brand operating guidance is generalized as universal law, or public rules are ignored when a tenant procedure is silent
the lane drifts into design authorship, compliance signoff, sourcing, costing, or shipment-release authority

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Customer or stakeholder requests a policy exception
Service request lacks the detail needed for safe resolution
Communication becomes a handoff to another lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Non-U.S. market destination rules for textile, footwear, and packaging labeling
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The public baseline here is anchored in U.S. FTC and CPSC requirements plus global size and chemical frameworks. Tenants shipping to the EU, UK, or other markets must supply destination-specific labeling and safety overlays at runtime.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Brand-specific sample-stage naming and approval thresholds
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public sources and named-industry guidance show common artifact types, but each tenant still needs to provide its own proto, fit, size-set, wear-test, PPS, TOP, or other approval-stage mapping and owner matrix.

notes: Authoritative records for this lane are the tenant's PLM, sample tracker, label-artwork system, compliance repository, ERP item master, and supplier portal, constrained by the public FTC, CPSC, ISO, GS1, ZDHC, and OEKO-TEX baselines listed above.
The lane stops before final fit approval, pattern engineering, sourcing approval, legal or regulatory interpretation, compliance signoff, and shipment or market-release decisions.
